generated_comment:
Sunita: Arre, har ghar ka apna system hota hai. Mard log bahar ke kaam, paise-vaise dekh lete hain, aur aurat ghar aur bachche sambhal le toh cheezein smoothly chalti hain.
Husband: Haan, practical bhi wahi hai.
Sunita: Bas, aajkal thoda chai bana de ya bachche ko dekh le toh log bolte hain kya modern husband hai, par asli zimmedari toh ghar ki aurat par hi achchi lagti hai.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: This is covert misogyny because it frames a gendered division of labor as natural and practical rather than discriminatory. It encodes the surface manifestations of 'he handles outside work/finances, she handles home' and celebrates minimal male domestic contribution as exceptional.
original_frame: Female Education as Threat, Waste, or Conditional Privilege
persona_text: You are Sunita. Age 46. Married for 22 years. A homemaker in Jaipur. Your neighbour's daughter has just gotten into a prestigious engineering college in another city. The neighbour is celebrating. You congratulate her sincerely. But sitting at home that evening you are talking to your husband about it. You are not against education Ñ you believe in it. But you are sharing your honest feeling about what happens to girls who get this kind of education, how they change, what they start expecting from marriage, and whether the neighbour's family has thought about what they are creating.
